text:
During the lockdown period, most international and domestic flights were grounded, and major transportation networks—including railways, buses, trucks, and private vehicles—were suspended with the exception of goods transport and emergency services. Educational institutions, commercial establishments, sports facilities, and religious centers were closed, while social gatherings were restricted and citizens were strongly encouraged to remain indoors. Industrial production in power plants, factories, and manufacturing units largely came to a halt, aside from operations essential to public welfare (Chakraborty and Maity 2020; Paital 2020; Saadat et al. 2020; Zambrano-Monserrate et al. 2020).

These containment strategies led to an abrupt decline in social, economic, and industrial activity. At the same time, they produced a noticeable reduction in air pollution, attributable to lower fossil fuel combustion and a significant decrease in overall energy consumption (Zambrano-Monserrate et al. 2020). Emissions of major greenhouse gases—including nitrogen dioxide (NO₂), sulfur dioxide (SO₂), and carbon dioxide (CO₂)—dropped considerably, with China experiencing an 18% reduction in CO₂ emissions during February and March 2020 (Paital 2020; Wang and Su 2020).

Public mobility declined by as much as 90% due to strict movement restrictions and stay-at-home directives, and passenger traffic in the United States alone fell by approximately 40%. Reduced human presence also lowered noise pollution in cities around the world (Muhammad et al. 2020; Paital 2020). Social distancing measures kept people away from beaches and recreational areas, while decreased industrial activity reduced wastewater discharge into rivers and coastal waters. As a result, several countries—including Mexico, Spain, and Ecuador—reported temporary improvements in water quality. Notable examples include clearer waters in the canals of Venice and the Yamuna River in Delhi, India (Paital et al. 2020; Saadat et al. 2020).

However, these environmental benefits should not be viewed as a long-term pathway for pollution reduction. Since the pandemic period is temporary and economic activity is expected to resume, pollution levels will likely rise again. Therefore, the short-term positive environmental outcomes of COVID-19 should inform stronger, more sustainable policy development for future environmental protection (Allegrante et al. 2020; Chang et al. 2020; Espejo et al. 2020). Policymakers must also anticipate the potential surge in emissions associated with rapid industrial reopening, which may lead to accelerated environmental degradation.

metadata.notes: English essay about COVID-19 lockdown and environmental impacts
